data.title: Eurozone Flash PMI Hints At Rising Stagflation Risk Amid War In Middle East
data.description:
The flash Eurozone PMI is ringing stagflation alarm bells as the war in the Middle East drives prices sharply higher while stifling growth.

Record cost surge

March’s flash PMI survey data from S&P Global showed firms’ costs rising across the eurozone at the fastest rate for over three years amid the surge in energy prices and choking of supply chains resulting from the war in the Middle East.

Input prices increased at the fastest pace since February 2023, the rate of inflation accelerating to one of the greatest extents since data were first available in the late 1990s. The 10.6 point jump in the eurozone manufacturing input cost index was the largest on record, while the 5.2 point jump in the services input cost index was the largest on record, barring only the surge in costs seen in March 2022 during the pandemic.

Supply Shock

Supplier delays have meanwhile intensified to their highest since mid-2022, largely linked to shipping issues relating to the crisis in the Middle East. Although the delays are far less widespread than seen during the height of the pandemic, the supply chain disturbances have applied upward pressure to prices, which will only intensify further should the supply shock prove long-lasting, as will any adverse impact on production capacity arising from input shortages.

The feed-through of higher costs to selling prices has been muted so far, according to the flash PMI for March, though the survey’s selling price gauge has nonetheless already risen to its highest since February 2024 and at a level indicative of consumer price inflation accelerating close to 3%.

Growth close to stalling

Output growth has meanwhile slowed to near-stagnation thanks to a slump in business confidence and deterioration of new orders. Falling to a ten-month low of 50.5 in March, down from 51.9 in February, the PMI’s composite output index has fallen to a level indicative of eurozone GDP growth slowing to a quarterly rate of just under 0.1% in March.

The forward-looking indicators point to a heightened risk of further deterioration and potential downturn in the coming months. The drop in the flash PMI future output expectations index was the largest recorded since Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

Policy outlook

The outlook naturally depends on the duration of the war and any potential lasting impact on energy and supply chains, but the flash PMI data underscore how the European Central Bank is no longer in a “good place” with respect to growth and inflation, and will have to tread a cautious path with respect to policy in the face of a clear and rising risk of stagflation in the coming months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Inflation Spike And Growth Slump Signalled As Flash PMI Tracks War Impact In March
data.description:
The war in the Middle East hit the UK economy in March, stalling growth while driving inflation sharply higher. Output growth across manufacturing and services has slowed to a crawl as companies blamed lost business directly on the events in the Middle East, whether through heightened risk aversion among customers, surging price pressures, higher interest rates, or via travel and supply chain disruptions.

Inflationary pressures have surged higher on the back of rising energy prices and fractured supply chains. The acceleration in cost growth in the manufacturing sector was especially severe, being the sharpest since the depreciation of the sterling following Black Wednesday in 1992.

The full impact on inflation and economic growth depends not just on the duration of the war but also on the length of disruptions to energy markets and shipping, though March’s PMI numbers clearly underscore how downside growth risks and upside inflation risks have already materialised.

The Bank of England faces a challenging period where it will need to balance these growth and inflation risks when setting policy, seeking to dampen the potential for the inflation spike to become more engrained while ensuring a hawkish interest rate outlook does not exacerbate downturn risks.

Business growth hit by war outbreak

UK businesses reported a marked slowing of output growth in March as demand for goods and services fell for the first time since last November following the outbreak of war in the Middle East.

The PMI headline Composite PMI Output Index has slumped from 53.7 in February to a six-month low of 51.0 in March, according to the preliminary ‘flash’ reading. The latest reading is broadly consistent with GDP growth stalling in March, albeit with a modest 0.2% quarterly rise indicated for the first quarter as a whole.

Manufacturing output grew only marginally, posting its worst performance since production growth resumed five months ago, while service sector business activity rose at the slowest rate since last September.

War impact

The March flash PMI data, collected between 12-20 March, represent the first snapshot of business conditions after the outbreak of war in the Middle East on 28 February. Approximately one-third of all companies reporting a reduction in orders attributed the decline directly to the events in the Middle East, albeit often through varying channels.

In the service sector, reduced travel and tourism bookings were reported due to war-related travel concerns, while increased prices, higher interest rates and risk aversion were also cited as dampening demand, especially in financial services.

Manufacturers likewise reported a broad-based demand hit from the war, with customers often postponing or cancelling orders, though factories also cited the war’s disruption to supply chains. Supplier delivery times lengthened in March to the greatest extent since July 2022, when supply chains were affected by the pandemic.

Half of all companies reporting longer lead times attributed the deterioration to the events in the Middle East to some extent, often citing the need to divert shipping due to the conflict. March’s supply delays are by no means as severe as those seen during the height of the COVID-19 disruptions, though they are more pronounced than those seen during the escalation of Houthi Red Sea attacks in 2024.

Cost pressures highest for over three years

The biggest impact of the war, however, was on prices. Higher energy and fuel prices, the latter driving up freight and shipping costs, exacerbated existing upward price pressures – notably from rising wage costs – to push input cost inflation up to its highest since February 2023.

Manufacturers reported an especially steep jump in costs, the rate of increase hitting the steepest since October 2022. The acceleration in manufacturing input cost inflation during the month was the greatest recorded since sterling was ejected from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism in 1992. Service sector input cost inflation also accelerated, hitting an 11-month high, led by a surge in cost inflation among travel and transport companies.

Higher costs fed through to increased selling prices, with the rate of inflation across goods and services climbing to the highest since April of last year. At its March level, the PMI’s Output Prices Index is indicative of consumer price inflation accelerating to around 4.5%.

Confidence slips amid war worries

A further toll on the economy from the war appeared via a sharp deterioration in business optimism. Companies’ expectations about the outlook for the year ahead sank to the lowest since last June, when confidence had been hit by US tariff worries, dropping well below the survey’s long-run average. The deterioration marks a sharp reversal of the recovery of confidence seen at the start of the year, when business expectations had revived to a 16-month high.

Job losses accelerate

The loss of confidence led to further job losses in March, with employment dropping across manufacturing and services at a slightly increased rate. Jobs have been cut continually since October 2024, according to the PMI survey panel, focused primarily on the need to offset higher staffing costs introduced in the past two autumn Budgets.

Read the press release here.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: American Express: Fairly Valued Despite Spending Pressures (Upgrade)
data.description:
While shares of American Express (AXP) have been a moderate performer over the past year, gaining 8%, the stock has had a more difficult start to 2026. Indeed, shares are down over 20% from their highs as concerns have mounted about credit quality and spending on travel and leisure activity. Even as higher energy prices from the Iran War are likely to pressure these metrics, I view AXP as being better positioned than most other banks and consumer credit names, given its higher-income customer base. I last covered American Express in November, reiterating shares as a “Sell” given their valuation, and that call has played out with the stock down 11% since then. With shares below my $310 target and a changed macro environment, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Over the past month, we have seen a material rise in oil prices, and I expect the rise we have seen to be at least a 0.3% headwind to growth. Obviously, it is very difficult to know the full impact without knowing how long the conflict persists and when oil flows return to normal. Headlines oscillate daily if not hourly, but I remain concerned that oil may stay higher for longer. In general, I view higher oil prices as akin to a tax on consumers, and many consumers will likely have to cut back on other spending to offset the impact on their budgets.

A severe enough energy shock can even cause a recession, especially when the labor market is softening, as it has been. In that case, financials can also see greater credit losses as well. In American Express’s case, I am more concerned about the impact on spending than on defaults, as it caters to a higher-income customer base who should be better able to withstand an inflation shock. While some spending may be curtailed, credit losses should be less impacted.

Now, I would also note that the company enters this period of macro uncertainty coming off a period of strong results. In 2025, American Express grew adjusted EPS 15% to $15.38 on 10% revenue growth. While competition for high-spending customers is intense, its premium credit card offering continues to gain traction, especially with millennials and Gen Z.

Exiting 2025, consumer spending on AXP’s cards was very strong. As you can see below, spending was up 9%, equally strong in goods & services and travel & entertainment. Now, 26% of its billed business is in T&E (travel and entertainment), so AXP’s volumes are very dependent on this discretionary consumer spending. This is precisely the type of spending that we may see negatively impacted by higher oil prices. This past weekend we learned that United Airlines (UAL) plans to pare back its schedule given the impact of higher oil prices, and I doubt this will be the last capacity cut we hear from the industry.

It is important to note that AXP’s customers have been holding up better than the national consumer. In 2025, we saw consumers pull back from discretionary activity after years of affordability strains. After all, hotel RevPAR fell in 2025 for the first time since COVID. Despite this, AXP registered high single-digit growth, indicative of the “K”-shaped economy narrative. Its higher-income consumers are likely to continue spending at a higher pace than other consumers, as we are likely to see many simply save a bit less to offset higher energy prices rather than spend less.

Still, I now expect to see a deceleration in spending activity. AXP also has a strong small business franchise, and these businesses are likely to reduce some discretionary activity as well, in my view. The impact will be felt more clearly starting in Q2, but we could see spending slow to the 5-7% zone by H2 of 2026, in my view.

It is also notable that Gen Z and Millennials are now 36% of its business, with Gen Z powering a disproportionate amount of growth, whereas Boomers are growing just 2%. Whether these younger consumers prove quite as durable during an inflation shock remains to be seen. Nonetheless, the ability of AXP to gain such a foothold has been a clear positive and helped power substantial card growth.

Because of this, fee revenue should accelerate in 2026, especially thanks to its Platinum card refresh and increased fees. Now, given competition, the company is investing more in rewards, and that will likely offset some of the fee revenue growth. In 2025, customer expenses were 43% of revenue, and I expect it to fall in the 44%-45% range in 2026.

Credit quality has been remarkably stable over the past year, with delinquencies holding at just 1.3%. In February, American Express disclosed that delinquencies were 1.4%, flat month over month. This was also the same as a year prior and better than pre-COVID levels. There is no sign of trouble yet, and I expect delinquencies to run in the 1.3%-1.5% area this year. I expect AXP’s credit performance to outperform peers like Synchrony (SYF), and barring a moderate recession, I view the oil shock as more of a spending than a credit headwind for the company. It has reserved 2.9% of loans for credit losses, a level it kept steady through 2025. I view this reserve coverage as sufficient, especially as I do not expect delinquencies to materially worsen even in a downside economic scenario.

In March, American Express increased its dividend by 16%, and shares now offer a 1.3% dividend yield. Plus, the company continues to repurchase shares, and thanks to $5.3 billion of buybacks, its share count is down 2% over the past year. AXP’s tier 1 common equity (CET1) ratio of 10.5% is entirely consistent with its 10%-11% target, and I expect a similar pace of buybacks in 2026.

Interestingly, even with the pullback in shares, American Express still gets an “F” rating from Seeking Alpha’s valuation screener. AXP was trading at a substantial premium to peers a few months ago, and that has not changed. While shares have dropped considerably, the entire sector has given credit fears, meaning it is still relatively expensive. Now, I believe AXP merits a premium valuation to peers, given its premier brand, solid growth, lack of private credit exposure, and high-quality customer base.

Still, on a P/E basis, it is trading at a ~60%-80% premium, which is not cheap. However, it is interesting to note that AXP is trading essentially flat to its 5-year historic valuation, unlike a few months ago when it was even trading at a premium to its own history. In my view, there is no way to argue that AXP is a relatively cheap stock. That said, I view the “F” score as overly harsh. A good chunk of its premium valuation is structural in nature, and shares are now more in line with their own history. Valuation remains a clear source of caution for me, but after the pullback, I do not view it as being a conclusive stand-alone sell trigger anymore.

Alongside Q4 results, AXP also provided initial 2026 guidance for 9%-10% revenue growth and $17.30-$17.90 in EPS. Now, this guidance was provided a month before the war in Iran. All else equal, higher energy prices and travel disruption are negative, though the magnitude is still highly uncertain given the duration and severity of the energy price spike are still unknown. Back in November, I was targeting $17 in 12-month forward EPS. Given incremental macro headwinds, I am now targeting $16.90-$17.50 in 2026 EPS, as we may see slightly higher reserves and a deceleration in spending growth.

At $300, shares are 17.4x earnings, and shares have a ~3.7% capital return yield. With long-term growth in the mid-to-upper single digits thanks to its success in winning emerging consumers, I believe valuation is becoming more appropriate, and I no longer view shares as a sell. That said, the macro environment is weaker today than in November. Even with shares below my previous buy trigger, I am only upgrading AXP to a “Hold,” as a worsening of the oil situation may further pressure sentiment and spending patterns. At ~17x or in the $280s, I would be a buyer, but at this price, AXP is a suitable “Hold.” I believe the underperformance is now complete but would not rush to buy more shares here.

data.title: Academy Sports and Outdoors: Waiting For More Proof That Consumer Traffic Will Return
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) previously with an upgrade to a buy rating as the underlying demand has gotten better and the strategy of opening new stores is working. As for this update, I agree that a lot of things are moving in the right direction for ASO, but the one metric that still matters most has not fully turned yet: traffic. That is why I am still downgrading to hold for now.

4Q25 Earnings

ASO reported its Q4 2025 earnings last week, with net sales coming in at $1.7 billion, up 2.5% y/y. The growth came largely from new stores and higher tickets, as comparable sales fell 1.6%. The mix was transactions down 6.4% and tickets up 5.1%. What came out positive was that gross margin expanded 140 bps y/y to 33.6%, and that appears to be structural, as management said most of the expansion came from supply chain efficiency gains and also lapping costs from the previous year's port disruption. SG&A as a percentage of sales was up 70 bps y/y, driven by $21 million growth in SG&A, but the majority of that happened because of new-store growth. At the bottom line, adj. net income was $132.9 million, down 4.3% y/y, while adj. EPS was $1.97, up 0.5% y/y.

The Operating Base is Improving

While I am staying neutral on ASO, I do think there are improvements that are worth highlighting. I will start with the digital side of things. Traction here is great, as ASO's e-commerce business grew 13.6% in FY2025 (way better than the headline revenue growth figure), and management said the reasons were better search, a better site experience, and the launch of Scout (AI assistant). These are practical changes that can improve how customers shop. Specifically, better search makes it easier to find the right product. A better site experience reduces friction. And if Scout helps shoppers navigate the site more effectively, that should support conversion as well. This matters for understanding ASO's digital segment growth path ahead because digital growth driven by a better shopping experience is likely more repeatable than growth driven by one-off promotions.

Loyalty and customer mix are improving as well. myAcademy Rewards now has more than 13 million members, and more importantly, traffic from households earning more than $100,000 grew 10%, and this group is now ASO's largest and fastest-growing customer cohort. A bigger loyalty base helps too, as it gives ASO a larger pool of engaged customers. The more important point, though, is the mix shift. Higher-income shoppers are usually less pressured by inflation, so demand from these customers should hold up better when the macro backdrop gets choppy. For what it's worth, this does not make ASO immune to macro risk, but it does make the business more resilient than before.

Traffic is Still the Part That Needs Proof

That said, the problem is that the demand signal is still not clean. The one key metric that outweighed everything else is that transactions fell 6.4%, which I think is still the core debate in ASO's equity story. Digital is doing well, loyalty programs and in-store efforts are showing their worth, but the customer has yet to come back. To be fair, there were some one-offs that made the story sound better. Management said that because of the storms in January, they shut roughly half the store base for about three days, and that created a ~100 bps comp headwind in Q4 2025. Excluding those days, management said ASO would have been running a positive comp in the mid-single-digit range. I am not sure how to reconcile that mid-single-digit range comment since reported comparable sales growth was down -1.6% in Q4 2025. Anyway, it's fair to say comparable sales growth would have been better, but this is something to monitor for the next quarter to see if it's true.

On this note, management's early read on 2026 was good. They said the first seven weeks of the Q1 2026 quarter were running positive, February was positive across every division, and that strength continued into early March. Those comments were in line with Bloomberg's alternative data, and assuming there are no major macro events (like the US/Iran conflict getting worse or anything like a pandemic), there is a good chance for this demand momentum to continue. This is especially with upcoming catalysts like higher tax refunds, the World Cup, and patriotic summer demand tied to the 250th anniversary of the US.

Valuation

While a bunch of strategic initiatives are working out, like loyalty programs, RFID, and the customer mix moving in the right direction, the key metric that anchors the entire thesis, traffic, is still weak. This was the risk that I flagged previously, and sadly, it is weighing on the stock narrative. The good news is that management upgraded their guide, now expecting adj. EPS of $6.10 to $6.60 from the prior range of $5.65 to $6.15. Note the high end of the previous guide is now near the low end of the current guide. I believe this revised guidance has given the stock multiple some support (now still at ~8.1x NTM PE), but my worry is that if traffic continues to stay weak, ASO may miss this guide, and that would be a disaster for the stock.

The last few times ASO missed its guidance by a big margin, the stock fell between ~5% and as much as ~11%, for reference. I would give it one or two more quarters to see how the traffic trends before I turn bullish again.

Conclusion

I downgrade to a hold rating. ASO is clearly doing several things right. Digital traction is improving, new stores are performing well, and the customer base is becoming more resilient. The problem is that traffic is still weak, and until ASO can show cleaner comparable sales improvement, I am not ready to upgrade to a buy rating yet. I would rather wait for another one or two quarters of proof before changing my view.

data.title: Apple Stock Price Firms Near $252 After WWDC Date Steadies Sentiment
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Apple (AAPL) shares traded on firmer footing Tuesday, March 24, with AAPL moving back toward $252 after an early dip held above $249, even as Treasury yields stayed elevated and the broader technology trade remained uneasy. The stock did not break decisively higher, but it stopped behaving like a market under forced liquidation, which mattered after the previous session’s pressure.

Apple spent much of the session repairing Monday’s softness rather than launching a clean breakout. Trading between roughly $250 and $253 left the stock in a narrow recovery channel, with buyers willing to defend the lower edge of the range but not yet aggressive enough to force a move through nearby overhead supply.

That leaves $250 in the foreground. A hold above that level keeps the recent pullback looking more like a reset inside a broader range than the start of a deeper unwind, while the first ceiling now sits around $253 and then again near $255, where recent rallies have lost traction.

Momentum, for now, looks restrained rather than broken. The stock is no longer trading as if sellers fully control the tape, but the rebound still has a provisional feel because every push higher is unfolding against a rate backdrop that remains unfriendly to expensive technology names.

A steadier Apple in a noisier market

The macro setting stayed difficult. Treasury yields climbed again on Tuesday, with the 10-year near 4.38% and longer-dated yields also pressing higher, as markets continued to price a stickier inflation backdrop and a heavier supply picture in fixed income.

That pressure did not hit every corner of the market equally. The Nasdaq lagged the broader tape, and both technology and communication services remained among the softer groups, which helps explain why Apple could stabilize without attracting the kind of momentum buying that usually follows a clean dip rebound.

What kept the stock from looking heavier was the return of a company-specific marker. Apple confirmed that its annual developers conference will run from June 8 to June 12, giving investors a fresh date around which software and artificial intelligence expectations can build, even if the immediate trading impulse still came from rates rather than product excitement.

Scenarios from here

A constructive path would keep AAPL above $250 and gradually reopen the way to $253 and $255. If yields stop climbing and the market becomes more willing to pay for duration again, Apple has enough relative strength to look stable before it looks fast.

The weaker scenario is simpler. A break back under $250 would expose the recent intraday low near $249 first, and from there, the market could start probing whether last week’s support zone closer to the mid-$240s needs to be tested again.

Apple still carries unusual weight in index behavior because it sits at the intersection of consumer demand, mega cap technology leadership and rate sensitivity. When the stock steadies under pressure, it often says as much about market tone as it does about Cupertino itself.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Booking Holdings: Market's AI Fear Creates A Strong Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Booking Holdings (BKNG), I highlighted how the recent pullback created an attractive opportunity backed by the company's strong leadership position, great financial health, and long-term tailwinds, despite macro uncertainty and fear.

With the stock getting even more attractive as the market is pricing in even more macro pressure and AI risk that seem to be overblown, I'm upgrading Booking to a Strong Buy, as the company stands to benefit significantly from AI integration, a potential travel recovery, their expansion beyond classic accommodation, and more.

Strong Performance Continues

Although Booking reported an overall mixed Q4, missing the market's EPS estimates despite beating on revenue, they delivered a 16% YoY increase in revenue and a 17% increase in diluted EPS during the quarter, although marketing expenses jumped by 22%.

During the whole year, they reported a more moderate 13% increase in revenue but a stronger 22% jump in EPS, backed by significant buybacks alongside their 17% profit growth, delivering margin improvements, room night growth, solid developments for Genius, and significant growth in alternative accommodations.

Meanwhile, they also beat their cost savings target, delivering $250 million worth of savings compared to their $150 million initial commitment despite an increase in costs. As a note, since I know this has been a subject for discussion for a long time, the company also announced a 25-to-1 stock split effective on April 2, 2026, getting them to a price of the equivalent of $175.88 today, which should help them reach a broader amount of investors, particularly retail ones that may not have access to fractional shares - although the real effect on the stock or the company remains meaningless.

Regarding the cash flow, adjusting it for stock-based compensation and deferred merchant bookings, as argued in the previous analysis, we see it more than doubling compared to 2023, reaching $7.67 billion in 2025 compared to $5.93 billion in 2024 and $3.73 billion in 2023.

During 2025, Booking also paid ~$1.25 billion in dividends alongside a strong $6.44 billion in buybacks, meaning a payout ratio of almost exactly 100% based on my Adjusted FCF, which would translate into a combined yield of roughly 5.5% based on today's price, which is not bad given the type of growth they can deliver alongside it.

For 2026, Booking expects low double-digit growth in gross bookings and revenue, while the Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted EPS would grow even faster, as expected, with the latter being projected to grow in the mid-teens, in line with their long-term EPS growth ambition of 15%.

Financially, based on Booking's latest report, we continue to see a strong position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities and a very significant amount of cash and cash equivalents available, providing a lot of flexibility if needed during the current environment and supporting even higher yields.

Still a Questionable Macro Landscape

Although we could see a boost from the FIFA World Cup and major events such as America's 250th celebration, that may be short-lived, as the ongoing Iran conflict has the potential of spreading into much more than just higher fuel prices.

Ultimately, we're talking about a conflict that affects much more than the oil and gas industry (which is vital for travel as well, I might add), with the jump in fuel prices visible from day one of the conflict ultimately representing a risk for travel budgets, while a prolonged conflict can rapidly turn into significant inflation and rising risks of a recession.

This also pushed the Fed into avoiding a cut recently, warning of the potential inflationary effects of the Middle East conflict, although I argued before that I don't see it as something that will last for that long given the significant implications it has on the global economy and the likelihood of achieving meaningful change, with Trump's recent pause following positive talks (which Iran refuted) being a step in the expected direction.

Meanwhile, political pressure can cause even more trouble, with the DHS temporarily suspending the PreCheck and Global Entry programs not long ago due to funding issues, potentially hurting US travel even more, although Booking has the massive advantage of being a global player, with branches such as Agoda allowing them to capture the high-growth SEA region, which could benefit even more from redirected travel interest, although the industry was already recovering and growing well even before this.

Regarding the AI disruption that seems to have caused significant worries in this industry in recent months, we see some positive developments, with OpenAI reportedly scaling back their direct checkout ambitions, with one analyst from Mizuho even saying that this switch from ChatGPT checkout into apps on ChatGPT can be the end of this bear pressure. In fact, given their massive amounts of data, traffic, merchants, and ability to integrate their services, I wouldn't be surprised to see a scenario where this would benefit companies like Booking in the long term.

Not to mention that directly integrating this kind of data from the merchants and allowing people to cut out the online travel agency middleman would require entire business structures dedicated to it, while people may not be willing to leave very important and time-sensitive details in the hands of what are otherwise overall unreliable AIs that can misinterpret data and ruin the entire vacation from the start.

Similar to BofA, which stated that these fears are overblown, Morgan Stanley also believes that Booking has little to fear from agentic AIs, recently upgrading them to Overweight:

We see OTA's ability to remain the merchant of record and still capture consumer browsing and purchasing data as being key to their long-term business…as in effect, we think the stage is setting up for BKNG and OTAs to remain just as critical to the long-term online travel landscape as they are now.

Meanwhile, one BTIG analyst highlighted that despite Wyndham recently striking partnerships with Google, Anthropic, and ChatGPT, Booking itself has limited exposure to chain hotels, and I'll add that it's this merchant diversity that offers a massive advantage for them, as many of these hotels/owners don't even have an online presence outside of these online travel agencies.

In fact, Booking's CEO and CFO commented extensively about how this can actually benefit them as well during their Q4 Earnings Call, highlighting the early results seen from implementing agentic AI:

I think a colleague CFO, said during our call last quarter, I've forgotten who it was, GenAI is everywhere except for in the P&L. I'm very proud that we can point to a line item in the P&L where we actually do have meaningful results on the efficiency basis because as we said during the call, our absolute number in terms of customer service costs are down and our bookings are up approximately 10%. So we have about a 10% decline in customer service cost per booking. And that has a real impact in our S&O and our sales and other line where we have seen now more leverage in the recent past.

Risks Reiterated

Although AI fears may be overblown, investors shouldn't ignore the current issues, which have the potential of turning this into a very bad environment for travel, with significant consumer weakness, especially in the US and China, even before the Iran conflict, which can directly affect Booking for longer.

This comes on top of potential competitive pressure (even outside of AI) that can hurt their margins, especially since Booking is trying to expand their offer and essentially "lock" the entire trip inside their own ecosystem.

Valuation

For Booking's DCF to Equity valuation, without adjusting this for net borrowing changes since I want to capture their pure cash generation potential and they should be able to deliver these results without significant balance sheet changes, I believe the previous growth assumptions still hold, getting us to an Adjusted FCF of roughly $8.82 billion in 2026 (excluding stock-based compensation and deferred merchant bookings).

Assuming a CAGR of 15% until 2030 - in line with their EPS estimate - possible thanks to potential cost savings from AI/others, expansion beyond hotels, a recovery in global travel and growth in SEA, followed by a CAGR of 8% for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate, as the company should be able to capture significant growth from certain regions, with a more aggressive discount rate of 10% this time to take into account the competitive risks and macro uncertainty, as well as 32.29 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report (before the 25-to-1 stock split).

This way, we get to an equity value of $217.83 billion, for an intrinsic value of $6,746.14 (or about $269.85 after the split), well above the current $4,313.46.

Conclusion

I'm upgrading Booking to a Strong Buy, as the company keeps proving their strength even during a relatively weak environment, being able to grow thanks to several internal and macro tailwinds.

There are risks, especially from the competition and AI, but these same issues are what got us to a good price in the first place, while the company can also stand to benefit from agentic AI, a recovery in travel, expansion beyond accommodation, and more.

data.title: How Global Tensions Are Reshaping U.S. Equity Risk
data.description:
By Vijay Sumon

U.S. equities returned 16%* in 2025, fueled by the AI boom, resilient consumer spending and three straight rate cuts toward the end of the year. As markets move through 2026, investor attention remains fixed on shifting geopolitical tensions – from Venezuela to Greenland to Iran – and the upcoming transition of the Federal Reserve (Fed) Chair from Powell to Warsh this May.

Also in focus are uncertainties surrounding trade policy, the U.S. labor market, inflation and the U.S. mid-terms, all of which serve as potential catalysts for market volatility.

How Does Geopolitical Volatility Impact Market Volatility?

Any shifts in geopolitical flashpoints can affect market sentiment and lead to repositioning as investors respond tactically. This dynamic was evident in January, when short-dated at-the-money (ATM) implied volatility in E-mini S&P 500 (ES) options – as represented on a rolling seven-day basis – rose from 9.75% to 11.31% (+1.57%) in response to U.S. action in Venezuela. Similarly, that same volatility metric spiked from 10.49% to 16.50% (+6.01%) following U.S.-EU trade tensions over Greenland.

While some investors responded by hedging portfolios via a pronounced shift to defensive strategies, others sought opportunity in navigating a new geopolitical environment.

A look at how investors positioned themselves in January using equity index options – as represented by the short-dated January and February, followed by March and June 2026 expiries – reveals a distinct evolution in sentiment.

While the market began January with a "protection" mindset across all maturities, by January 30 positioning had shifted. Immediate risks were largely ignored in favor of aggressive upside speculative targets.

Short-term positioning was heavily defensive, characterized by significant hedging (put options) at lower levels. However, as the timeline extended into the March and June quarterly expiries, sentiment shifted significantly toward a bullish outlook, with traders positioning for upside targets above 8000.

June 2026 Investor Positioning Reveals Lingering Concerns

As of March 4, the market positioning for the June maturity indicates that some traders are preparing for potential volatility by establishing deep out-of-the-money (OTM) hedges at the 4850 and 5000 level, indicating increased institutional demand for downside protection following the geopolitical developments this year. Conversely, bullish speculators are targeting a move toward the 7400–8100 range.

Given the density of events that have occurred only a few weeks into 2026, the ability to adjust exposures in real time – during U.S. and non-U.S. trading hours – remains critical for investors.

The average daily volume for equity index options outside of U.S. trading hours reached over 200,000 contracts as of December 2025, accounting for 16% of the total daily equity options volume, as global institutions increasingly manage their risk around the clock.

How Do Volatility Levels Compare to Last Year?

As 2026 pans out, June ATM implied volatility is again below its long-term average and trading in-line with levels similar to that of 2025. Trade policy and geopolitical uncertainty in the first half of 2025 caused options premiums and ATM implied volatility to rise. Between March and April 2025, when tariffs were announced on Liberation Day (April 2, 2025), June 25 ATM implied volatility peaked at around 34%.

As market conditions stabilized, implied volatility then moved lower to around 15%, below its long-term average range of 16%-19%. Current 2026 levels appear to be following a similar stabilization pattern for now.

Even so, this year is shaping up to be a period of potential heightened volatility, driven by a complex interplay of economic and geopolitical factors. Options on equity index futures offer a versatile toolkit to navigate this environment.

Whether hedging risk, generating income or positioning for volatility shifts, these instruments provide investors with the flexibility to adapt their strategies as market conditions evolve.

*as measured using the S&P 500 Index

Original Post

data.title: Pennsylvania Municipal Bond Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 1.88% (Institutional shares) and 1.81% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • Long duration (high interest rate sensitivity) and yield curve positioning, coupled with strong selection in the health care, transportation, and education sectors, drove the fund's positive performance. Idiosyncratic positions and modest allocation effects, including underweight positions in the tax-backed sectors, detracted amid outflows at quarter-end and market volatility.
  • The fund had overweight positions in long maturity and positively convex (the rate at which duration changes in response to interest rate movements) bonds, and maintained significant allocations to the health care, transportation, and education sectors. It had underweight positions in short maturity bonds and tax-backed state credits, which reflected a focus on yield, credit quality, and sector resilience.

Contributors

Strong security selection in the health care, transportation, and education sectors was the primary driver of positive performance. The fund's long duration and yield curve positioning benefited from the Federal Reserve's (Fed) dovish policy shift and the subsequent rate rally. An exposure to resilient Pennsylvania credits and diversified allocations across essential service sectors further supported returns. Active management of portfolio structure and reinvestment of cash flows maximized income during a period of robust supply and demand and favorable credit conditions.

Detractors

Weak performance was primarily driven by underweight positions in bonds maturing in 15 or fewer years, which performed well during the quarter. Modest negative contributions also resulted from select sector allocations, particularly an underweight exposure to tax-backed state bonds. Additionally, short call bonds performed weakly in the market rally, which further detracted from results.

Further insight

The fund's solid quarterly return was supported by strategic positioning and active sector allocation. Its long duration bias and yield curve exposure were key contributors as rates rallied following the Fed's dovish pivot. The fund held minimal cash as it remained fully invested, which maximized income. At quarter-end, positioning emphasized resilient sectors and positively convex bonds. Management remains constructive on Pennsylvania credits and will continue to emphasize active sector rotation and credit selection in response to the evolving market conditions.

References

  2. 2 The BBG Custom Pennsylvania Municipal Bond Index measures the performance of municipal bonds issued in Pennsylvania.

The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. data.title: Gold Under Pressure - Why The Next Leg Higher Has A New Catalyst
data.description:
Two Channels, One Shock

Gold has declined sharply since the U.S.–Iran war triggered an oil price surge, with prices almost erasing year-to-date gains. Two distinct transmission channels explain the move, both originating from the oil shock.

Higher real rates

The real rate is the nominal bond yield minus inflation expectation, and it was once the primary long-run anchor for gold. The U.S. 10Y nominal bond yield (US10Y) has been on the rise since early March, moving up from 3.96% to 4.39% as a result of the escalating Middle East conflict fueling energy prices and renewed inflation concerns.

Similarly, the 10Y breakeven inflation rate has also moved up from 2.25% to 2.38% during this period of time, resulting in the 10Y TIPS (measure of real interest rates) moving up from 1.70% to 2.00%. Gold generates no income; when the risk-free rate rises, the opportunity cost of holding gold rises, and capital rotates out. The mechanism is consistent across cycles.

However, the relationship has weakened since 2022. Gold continued to rise alongside higher real yields during 2022–2025, supported by strong central bank buying, geopolitical risk hedging demand, and persistent U.S. fiscal deficits.

Petrodollar funding squeeze

While the long-term inverse relationship between gold and real yields still carries some explanatory power, it has materially weakened since 2022. This suggests a second channel is playing a large role in the current gold price decline: a global dollar funding squeeze.

Oil-importing economies, including China, India, Europe, Japan, and South Korea, collectively purchase about 70% of global crude. With oil prices up more than 40%, the dollar cost of the same energy volume has risen sharply. These countries must secure materially more dollars against export revenues and cash flows that adjust slowly.

The resulting demand shock for dollars is structurally inelastic: energy imports cannot be deferred, and adding to that is most cross-border liabilities are denominated in dollars with fixed servicing schedules, which still need to be paid on time. This dynamic creates a temporary dollar shortage, reflected in the surge in the dollar index.

This dynamic extends beyond the national level. Businesses and households also need additional cash flow to cover rising energy costs, with asset liquidation as the most immediate source.

Liquid assets, including gold and equities, are sold to raise dollars quickly. Gold’s high liquidity and global ownership make it a preferred source of emergency dollar funding, leading to a broad and rapid selloff.

Tokenized Gold's Rapid Ascent

The current selloff is a liquidity event, not a fundamental repricing. More critically, even if real rates remain somewhat elevated, the two fundamental driving forces for gold, sovereign debasement hedging and reserve diversification, are rate-insensitive.

Global central banks buying gold to reduce dollar exposure are less concerned about the 10Y TIPS yield. Investors buying gold to hedge the currency debasement risk and acting as an anchor in portfolios for store-of-value are not sensitive to short-term price fluctuations either.

And now there is a new catalyst. Store-of-value demand for gold has historically been capped by distribution friction. Physical gold requires custody and logistics, and paper gold, ETFs require a brokerage account and banking access that a large portion of the global population does not have. The total addressable market for gold has been limited by the infrastructure required to access it.

Tokenized gold removes that friction. Anyone with a mobile phone can acquire exposure to physically-backed gold without a bank account, broker, or custodian logistics. The total tokenized gold supply has grown rapidly since late 2025.

While the total size remains small at less than 40 tonnes compared to the above-ground gold reserve of approximately 216,265 metric tonnes according to the World Gold Council (2025), the growth rate cannot be neglected. In the past half year, the total supply of tokenized gold has doubled in quantity.

Tether Gold (XAUT-USD) and Paxo Gold (PAXG-USD) have dominated the market with more than 95% combined market share. The duopoly reflects a structural barrier to entry. Launching digital gold products requires issuers to independently build custody relationships, meet compliance and audit framework, and establish redemption logistics.

Recently, the largest global gold association, the World Gold Council, announced it is building a shared infrastructure to make digital gold products interoperable, scalable, and easier to launch. The proposed system is organized around three layers.

A physical layer would manage sourcing, storage, transport, and redemption of actual gold. A digital layer would handle issuance, ownership records, and product-lifecycle management. Finally, an interface layer would allow issuers to build their own customer-facing experiences on top of the shared stack. Under this model, issuers would compete on user experience, pricing, and distribution, not on custody infrastructure.

Sizing the Potential Market

To estimate the potential market size of tokenized gold, we can use the gold ETF market as a benchmark. Global gold ETFs reached 4,025 tonnes as of the end of 2025; tokenized gold today sits around 37 tonnes, less than 1% of the ETF size. The growth rate, however, is of a different order entirely: in 2H 2025 alone, the tokenized gold supply expanded over 100%.

Because tokenized gold targets new channels, such as retail users in emerging markets, rather than cannibalizing existing gold ETFs, gross token growth translates into incremental physical gold demand.

If the tokenized gold market sustains an 80-100% annual growth rate, cumulative net new physical demand could exceed 240 tonnes in the next five years, making it reach 7% of the current gold ETF market. Under a more moderate assumption of 50% annual growth, incremental demand would fall in the 100-140 tonnes range.

The WGC infrastructure initiative is additive to this trajectory, as the current observed growth has occurred entirely within the existing two issuers. A successful implementation of shared digital gold infrastructure could potentially expand the addressable market further, bringing a more optimistic estimation.

Bottom Line

The near-term gold weakness is a petrodollar funding event, where people seek additional dollar liquidity to service inelastic energy demand and debt obligation. The traditional relationship between gold and real interest rates may still exert some pressure on price, although this linkage has weakened significantly since 2022.

The fundamental drive for gold in recent years has been reserve diversification and debasement trade, led by sovereign demand and is rate-insensitive. The near-term selloff does not change that thesis.

Tokenized gold represents a new source of demand, where a new distribution channel capable of reaching 5+ billion people in emerging markets, who have strong store-of-value needs.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post
